does: This paper demonstrates that narrow domain finetuning of large language models creates identifiable biases in activation patterns that can be extracted and analyzed using model diffing techniques. The authors show that by computing activation differences between base and finetuned models on random text, they can recover signals about the finetuning domain. Through activation steering—adding these differences back to base model activations—they generate text matching the format and content of finetuning data. They validate this approach by building an LLM-based interpretability agent that achieves significantly better performance than baseline prompting methods at identifying finetuning domains across multiple scenarios including synthetic document injection, emergent misalignment, subliminal learning, and taboo word guessing tasks.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of mechanistic interpretability and model diffing, which studies how model internals change during training and fine-tuning. It extends prior research on activation steering and causal intervention in neural networks, as well as work on understanding how domain-specific knowledge gets encoded in model weights and activations. The paper connects to recent studies on detecting unintended model behaviors and understanding what information is captured during finetuning, responding to growing concerns about covert capability injection and model manipulation. It also relates to interpretability research that uses differences between models as a window into learned representations.
enables: This work opens directions for detecting and analyzing narrow finetuning in deployed or suspect models without access to training data, potentially improving model auditing and safety practices. It suggests that activation patterns may serve as high-fidelity fingerprints of finetuning interventions, enabling detection of unauthorized model modifications. The interpretability agent framework points toward automated systems for reverse-engineering model capabilities and biases. These findings could inform defenses against adversarial finetuning or enable better monitoring of fine-tuned model behavior in production. Additionally, the approach may generalize to understanding other forms of model adaptation beyond narrow finetuning, contributing to broader mechanistic understanding of how LLMs encode specialized knowledge.
